speaker: interviewer
text: Is the interviewer. August 15, 1995.

speaker: interviewer
text: And action.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you started to tell me another story now.

speaker: interviewee
text: The story's like this. See, when I had the little brush shop my-- and we had the Lithuanian from the city hall and the lady over from the city hall. A man, he was like the manager, you know, from this, appointed by the city to look over. And we need hair, you know, to make the brushes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of hair?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hair of-- horse hair. So we had to go from Shavl to Kovno to get the hair for the shop. So they took my brother. Well, my brother-- a Jew cannot go on a train, in a car, and he cannot even do nothing. So they allow them to go with this manager, the Lithuanian manager, to Kovno, over to Kovno. Come over to Kovno, he had to bring my brother in the Kovno ghetto. In Kovno was a ghetto. Sometime for Kovno ghetto, they used to come in in Shavl, by the-- later, you know, in Shavl, you know. The German used to make all kind of stuff. They need something. They used to go from city to city. They need sometime Jew, they used to take him. But they couldn't sleep in a hotel. They had to sleep in ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my brother was in there, when he find out my sister is there, the oldest sister, with three children. Her husband, they killed. And she was with three small children. And she had to go to work and no food, you know? It's bad condition, you know? I don't know how he managed. They allowed them to take my sister from Kovno ghetto to Shavl ghetto. We didn't know nothing. And he come over to the Shavl ghetto. It's a gate, you know, with police, you know? He come over a truck stop. I saw my brother was out. They had to bring him in Shavl ghetto from Kovno with a truck. And he had my sister on the [INAUDIBLE]. When he did it, it was the same like you put in space somebody. The biggest miracle in this world. Did your sister survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they killed her. They killed. I saw that with the-- the SS man took out the older kids. Two kids they took out. And one they put in cellar and they didn't find him. And after this, they sent over my sister with the kid to the airport while I was there, when she was with us. And the commandant-- there was a few kids, maybe three, four kids. And sometimes they used to come in and look for the kids. He used to take them out and put them in barrels, the commandant.

speaker: interviewer
text: The German commandant?

speaker: interviewee
text: The commandant. And they didn't find them, see. We had them all the time over. When they took out, on the end, when the Russian start to lose the war-- the German lose the war and the Russians come in, they took out all the people where they left over to Germany from Lithuania, from the ghettos.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was going with my sister [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and the two kids to the brother's first in a place in, I think, in Poland. Stutthof, they called this, a concentration camp. I was there, I don't know, for 20 hours. 20 hours. After 20 hours, they count up 3,000 people. And I was one with-- from the 3,000. They put us on the train. We was going maybe for two weeks, and they brought us near Munich.

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister with the kid, they want-- they took, I think, 200 people too, women, for the kitchen or for something else. They want to release her since she's got two of them, but she have to leave the kid. Somebody told me who survived in Israel, she was with my sister. So my sister said, you killed already two kids of mine. And I don't want the third one. So they took her, and I think they transport her. I didn't know this till four or five weeks ago. They brought her to Auschwitz, and they gassed her. Because I got the information from the Red Cross Geneva. They find out they did it. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: How long-- how long was she with you in the barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the barracks? Not too long. Maybe two, three months.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long altogether from the time your brother brought her from Kovno?

speaker: interviewee
text: Close to a year, no more.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Michla

speaker: interviewer
text: Michla. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] How long were you in the barracks altogether?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the barracks, I was maybe-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] maybe 3/4 year, no more.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when did they move you from the barracks to Stutthof? What year, what month?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was maybe in summertime. I think in [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in '44, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they transport you?

speaker: interviewee
text: First they brought us in the ghetto Shavl, and we were staying there for, I think, a day. And after the day, they took us from the ghetto to trains. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we was going, I don't know, for a long time. We was in Konigsberg, a German city, you know? We was going by train. Was, I don't know, 40 people in a train. The train was not for people, you know, the train for-- for cattle, maybe. Some of them didn't survive even in train. And they brought us to Stutthof.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you with your brother still at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your brother one of the 3,000 who was sent to Stutthof?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, my brother was-- when the-- in Shavl, on the airport. After this, they made him, Panevezys, a place like this. They took from this camp, I don't know a couple hundred people or a hundred people. My brother was with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So [? you-- ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: He was there, and we were separated.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were with--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they took him, too, see? They took people from Panevezys in trains to-- to Germany. But when they took the people there, I don't know how many people was there. Because they brought people from Hungary there who used to work on this field, all kind. My brother jumped from train. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When the German took him in train to go to Germany, he jumped out from train. And they looked for him with dogs. He was in the woods, I don't know, for not too many hours. And the Russian come over and they liberated him, see. So he didn't have to go. After, you see, he was working for the Russian government, you know, in Shavl. He was the engineer on the old city factories. And he had a lot of good things he did for Jewish people, you know. He, by himself, they sent out in Siberia. He was in Siberia for 10 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: After the war this was?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: After the war this was?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. This was in-- yeah, yeah, after the war. The Russian sent him out because they find out he want to leave the train. So they sent him out to Siberia. And in Siberia you can live for two, three weeks or a month, then you have to die the conditions is so bad. When he was there 10 years survivor, he became a medical doctor in camp. And he was the doctor of the camp. Beside the camp, from all the cities, the farms, used to come in, the private people to the doctor. He was the doctor. A lot of things he couldn't do by himself because he was not graduate from college. And a lot of things what we couldn't do here, that authority to give you a license to go to hospital. So they used to take you in the hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now, you were moved by cattle car to Stutthof, and you said you were only there one day.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: After a day, I thought that this day I-- I couldn't even survive this day, it was terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- how was it terrible?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. They put us on-- on the-- how would you call them-- a yard, cold. They say you have to have a-- a shower. When we come over with-- from the ghetto I had clothes. And I had even gold, I had. When I was in-- in the airport, I had gold chains and gold watches, you know? When I was working in the brush shop there and I had a carpenter shop, I made brackets that would hold shelves, you know. So over my bed where I sleep, I filled up with the gold and I had-- when I left, I had the gold and I brought it in Stutthof.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I saw it's no use. I was sitting near a shul, you know? I threw everything in the shul. But I saw they had prisoners where they used to go in the shul and take it up. [LAUGHS] But this is not [INAUDIBLE]. But the only thing is they say you need a shower. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The shower is-- I was cleaner than they are They took away all our clothes, naked. And they checked us in case we got something in our mouth or something else, you know, in our body, something valuable. They checked. After they checked us, they let us out from the other door, and they put us on without underwear. They took away. They put me on a suit from a French prisoner with blood, you know? And the shoes they took away. And on the other door, we was laying there, I don't know, for six hours on the ground. Cold, terrible cold. How'd you know it was a French prisoner?

speaker: interviewee
text: The uniform. I-- I recognized the uniform. It was marked something.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, like a soldier.

speaker: interviewee
text: A soldier.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: They killed the [INAUDIBLE] there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They put us in there. And after this, [BACKGROUND NOISE] they count up 3,000 people. They open up the gate, and we was going to the train. They put us on the train, and we was going. I didn't know where we were going anyway. And they brought us near Munich. Landsberg, a city. It's a big place where they take out sand, you know, for-- for cement work, you know, they dig, you know, like they got here. I thought, here is the place where they're going to kill us. [LAUGHS] No, after a few hours later, they-- they put us in little huts, you know, made round, you know, from paper, from cardboard. And they're round, you know. And in the middle is a stick, you know, and it's covered with a little roof. And there were 20 in that place. And they tell us to lay down like this, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: All around the stick?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, and it was wet even, the ground was wet. And we start to work this. They used to send us to work. We built a factory, Messerschmitt. Messerschmitt factory for the planes they had underground. They built underground. They had 50,000 prisoners working there.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were building the factory, or you were working on the planes?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. We were working on the factory. We built the factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: You built the factory, underground factory.

speaker: interviewee
text: Underground factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your job?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know, they used to send me-- I didn't-- sometime to-- to put with a shovel, you know, in there, cement. Who knows, every day, they sent us something else on. No food, you know? And we coming back to the-- to the places, you couldn't find even your place to-- to sleep. Somebody else was sleeping there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have enough to eat?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no food. I don't know how I survived. I had food-- when I was in ghetto, I had food because on my own, I organized. When I worked on the airfield, I had food. Over in Germany, nothing. They used to give us a piece of bread for all day long. And the bread, when they run away from the Germans, they took the bread with them back that they came over. The bread used to come in green, you know. They used to blow. They used to go out the green powder like--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mold.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mold. At first they used to give, I think, allow for bread for the German-- for the soldiers got like a little loaf of bread for three people or four people. After this, they gave you for six people, the same. Ah, it's no good. I don't know how I survived. No-- no food, nobody. And after this they had typhus epidemic and dysentery. They used to-- near me, they used to die. They used to close up the camp. They didn't go in. They didn't let us out because of typhus. They were afraid. And people died like flies there, and I don't know how I survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever get sick?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I was sick. I had pneumonia one time. They had me in a little place like a hut, I think, for a few days and they just beat me up there. They used to beat me, instead of to heal me. And, ah. It's-- it's a lot of trouble. [LAUGHS] But I don't know, so I have to survive, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: I told you before, when I started, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the news man started talking to him and I say to this secretary from our lodge. He said, since when did you become a celebrity? I didn't know what he was talking to me. They say you was on television. I find out what kind of television, there's cable television in [INAUDIBLE]. And they tell me all week, this and this time will be. So next day I put on the television. I saw myself. I want to know, what did I say? Because I didn't know. You didn't remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't-- I didn't know what I was talking to him. When they opened up the television, I heard what I said. They asked me, how do you survive? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I answered them how I survived? I say, the United States threw a bomb on Hiroshima and they killed so many hundreds of thousands of people. Six people survived to ask him how they survived. This way I know how I survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: After this, I told them I bought a house in Salem. And there's flies in yard. I bought a gallon of DDT. And I started to spray out DDT. DDT is poison, you know. They cannot use it even now. And I kill a lot of flies, but some flies stay alive. They fly away. [LAUGHS] Then the same with me. He asked me how I survived. Who knows how I survived? I survived. Who knows? How long were you in-- in Landsberg?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Landsberg I was maybe eight months, nine months.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did-- how did you stop? What-- what ended that-- that period?

speaker: interviewee
text: The end of this period? When the-- the Germans started to lose the war and the Americans were already near Landsberg-- near-- it's close to Bavaria, you know? And this Landsberg, while I was in this camp supervised by Dachau. See, it was 11 camps. Before the German lost the war, the American came in. This is the same like they did before from Lithuania. They took us to Dachau.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Germans took you to Dachau. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, from the camps, from the 11 camps. Dachau's not too far, maybe 10 miles. We was walking, though. On the way they was out for nice [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] cars, you know, Mercedes, nice clothing, you know, people. And they had a speech for us. We're taking you to Switzerland to exchange on German prisoners. They could tell us anything. What can you do? They brought us there.

speaker: interviewer
text: To Dachau.

speaker: interviewee
text: To Dachau. And Dachau was not like it used to be. So I never saw Dachau. I was there just a day, I think, maybe more, a little bit more now. To diminish a bit. The grass must be a little runaway, you know? On the inside there was no order because they had-- you see, when they-- in Dachau they brought must be 50 million prisoners in this time, in the period from Hitler. When the-- seemed like when I was coming over from Stutthof. You come in with suitcases. They take away everything from you, and they-- they piled up. And after this, they covered this with waterproof cloth, you know. And after this they sort out, you know, and they send out to the people when they get bombed out in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: When they brought us over, there was Russian prisoners there. And they are a little bit tough, you know. So they start to open up the bundles, you know, the suitcases. And they put on clothes, you know? They could do a lot of vile things, you know? Couldn't do this before. And before we left, we was there for a day, and after this, they gave us food, you  know? I don't know, a can of meat and a-- and a piece of bread. This is still the Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the German in Dachau. And they say we're taking out you to exchange on Swiss prisoners. So we was going-- we was going five miles, no, two miles, you know? They used to see the Americans in front. They used to go back. They used to go back and forth. They took us out 35,000 prisoners. And from the 35,000 prisoners, till we were liberated this took maybe a-- a week or five days. 4,000 survived. I was one from the 4,000. They wanted to take us to Tyrol, this is in Austria, in the hills, and shoot us. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] And I survived this too. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see the Americans? When they-- when the Americans came, where were you? Back in Dachau?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was not back in Dachau. I was-- I was liberated in Bad Tolz, not too far from Dachau, not too far from Tyrol. A little farm, you know. I was liberated there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Still on the march.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the march, yeah. I was liberated. After this I saw the Americans. And the American come in, you know, to the-- there was empty houses. They put us in places, you know, to sleep.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about when you saw the Americans.

speaker: interviewee
text: By the liberation I didn't saw. But the only thing is-- I don't know, the next day or in-- maybe the same day, I was out and the American Army with the tanks, they was going in the street. And I never saw American soldier, nothing. So I was interested to look, I was looking, you know. Americans.

speaker: interviewer
text: I saw a jeep stop. A jeep stops and he goes over to me, and I was with the-- with the clothes, you know, with the stripes, you know, and even torn, you know? He goes over to me and he says, [? Lanceman. ?] Nice boy, tall, you know, blond. I thought he looks like a German, you know? When he say [? lanceman ?] I don't know what's he saying. What are you talking to me? In English, I couldn't understand one word and I couldn't talk one word. They say, are you a Jew? [INAUDIBLE]? I say, yeah. He couldn't speak a few words, you know? They became friendly with me, you know? So they put me in a camp for displaced persons, you know? In his spare time he used to come in and visit me, you know? And I was very friendly with him. What's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Milton Endlich. He's from New York, Brooklyn, New York. And after this [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] he was-- even come over to my son's bar mitzvah, he come over. I find out about him. Now he's in-- in New York. But now he became sick and he had to take off a leg. He had-- what do you call it-- diabetes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Diabetes. So he befriended you. What did he do for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not much. In his spare time, he used to come in and talk to me. Sometimes he used to bring me something, you know? By this time we had food, you know? The United Nations used to supply us with [BACKGROUND NOISE] meals. But it was the same. After this he asked me if you've got relatives in the United States. I say, the only thing what I know, my father had a brother. On New Year, he had one time a greetings card, you know, New Year's Day, something like that. I remember, I was maybe 10 years old at this time when I saw this little card. And I remember the-- the name and the address and the street, I remember. And nobody could write letters, just the American soldiers-- he said, I will send it. Write a letter. I will send a letter. He sent a letter to-- it's a cousin. My-- my uncle my father's brother, he died but he had sons. The one son answered. [BACKGROUND NOISE] He answered.

speaker: interviewer
text: He say, how can you prove you're a cousin? Because I had a little bit idea, too. The picture I'll show you. I remembered my father sent them a picture from the family. I told him, if you've got this picture, you will see I'm on this picture holding hands like this. After they see, he answered me. And he say, I saw the picture. We got the picture, and you are there. They sent me out an affidavit to come to United States.

speaker: interviewee
text: I registered. Well, if I would be smart enough, and I didn't know nothing, I would register I'm born in Germany, I would go in two weeks. But I registered I am born in Lithuania. Lithuania got four people a year to come in and 275,000 registered. So I cannot come [LAUGHS] in. So after this, Truman made a law. Then after a [? quarter ?] for these people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I come over through United Nations. So I'm thinking, people who come in through the United Nations they didn't give a name from a relative, they gave them a hotel and a job. When I gave a name, so they put me on a train like in Germany. And they sent me over to this bus. I come over and I didn't have luck with my cousin. I had a couple days and after this, they didn't want nothing to do with me. But I survived, you know. [LAUGHS] Let me go back to Germany after the war. You stayed-- you said that you were liberated in this town, Bad Hills, is that right? Is that-- that's where you were liberated?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where?

speaker: interviewer
text: You said you were liberated in a place called Bad Hills?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bad Tolz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Bad Tolz. When you were there, did you live-- you said they-- they cleaned out some houses from the Germans and gave them to the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they didn't clean out. Some-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a German soldier go over, there was a group, maybe five, 10, you know? There was a lot of them, you know, a lot of soldiers and a lot of groups. I was in a group five, 10 people. We were staying there. The soldier, an American soldier come over and he say, come with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he saw an empty house, an empty house. When they put us in the house to sleep, in a-- he gave us a bed, I think, it was a bed, I think, for two people. I was in a bed. When I was out from bed. I couldn't sleep in a bed because I'm not accustomed. I laid down on the floor. I could sleep. In the bed, I couldn't. How about-- actually, we're going to change the tape in a second. Let me just ask you one other question, though. With food, were you careful about what you ate right away? Because a lot of times--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is where you ask till you know something. You're a good correspondent, you know? [LAUGHS] See, a lot of people-- not a lot. I don't know a lot-- even it's a few. See, the American soldiers, they threw out from the jeeps, from their trucks, food, you know, sardines, I don't know what. Some people grabbed right away, they opened up, they ate, and a half hour later they died. I was lucky nobody threw out to me, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: The only thing-- one time, a soldier brought us over to a house, and he asked the lady from the house-- there was five people, I think-- feed them. I don't know, she said something. She hasn't got something or what. So he opened up the stove. We took out [BACKGROUND NOISE] the dinner and put on the table there. Because potatoes, the potatoes didn't kill me, you know? And I would eat something wrong, I wouldn't be killed. [LAUGHS] Good. OK, we'll change the tape.
